feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Personal website of Sumantrak Mukherjee, focusing on causal inference, sequential decision-making, and spatio-temporal event modeling.

entry.title: Designing Objective Functions for Multi-Armed Bandits

link.title: Designing Objective Functions for Multi-Armed Bandits

entry.content:
Designing Bandit Objective Functions

The core of any bandit algorithm is the design of its objective function — this governs how the learner balances exploration and exploitation. Most classic objectives are cast as forms of regret minimization, but modern applications demand broader criteria.

🔧 General Design Principles

Objective functions are typically crafted based on:

  • Information structure: What kind of feedback do we get (numeric, ordinal, full reward vector, preference comparisons)?
  • Decision horizon: Are we optimizing over a fixed number of rounds or indefinitely?
  • Application goals: Maximize total reward? Identify the best arm? Ensure fairness? Minimize risk?

🎯 Classical Objectives

Objective Definition Use Case
Cumulative Regret $R_T = T \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^T \mathbb{E}[\mu_{a_t}]$ Long-term revenue, ad placement
Simple Regret $R = \mu^* - \mathbb{E}[\mu_{\hat{a}}]$, where $\hat{a}$ is the selected best arm Pure exploration, scientific discovery
PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) Find an arm within $\epsilon$ of optimal with probability $\ge 1 - \delta$ Safe decision-making, high-stakes domains

⚖️ Modern and Domain-specific Objectives

Objective Description Domains / Examples
Risk-sensitive regret Penalize high variance or downside loss Finance, healthcare
Fairness-aware regret Equal opportunity or demographic parity across arms Hiring, lending
Budgeted objectives Maximize reward under cost/resource constraints Mobile devices, sensor networks
Multi-objective trade-offs Optimize several competing metrics simultaneously Recommendation systems
Robust regret Worst-case regret under model misspecification or adversarial reward shifts Safety-critical applications
Information gain Maximize knowledge about arm means (e.g. Thompson Sampling variants) Adaptive experimentation

Objective Functions by Bandit Type

We now briefly connect different bandit types with typical objectives and design constraints.

Bandit Type Common Objective Function(s) Comments
Stochastic MAB Cumulative regret UCB, TS, ε-greedy apply
Best-arm Identification Simple regret / PAC Focused on exploration
Combinatorial Bandits Submodular reward or structured regret Require arm composition models
Contextual Bandits Contextual regret Linear, neural reward functions
Dueling / Ordinal Bandits Preference-based regret Partial feedback, pairwise comparisons
Causal Bandits Interventional regret Causal graphs, do-calculus guided
Multi-objective Bandits Pareto regret, weighted-sum regret Trade-offs in reward dimensions
Adversarial Bandits Regret vs worst-case comparator EXP3, minimax approaches
Batch Bandits Batch regret, regret per round Limits adaptivity
Private Bandits Regret under privacy constraints Adds noise, affects confidence bounds
Risk-aware Bandits CVaR or tail-risk regret Tailor exploration to risk profile
Principle Idea Examples
Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty Prefer arms with uncertain but potentially high rewards UCB, LinUCB
Bayesian Reasoning Maintain and update belief distributions over rewards Thompson Sampling, Bayesian UCB
Minimax / Adversarial Framing Prepare for the worst-case reward scenario EXP3, Robust UCB
Information-Directed Sampling Balance information gain with expected regret IDS, Bootstrap TS
Structural Exploitation Leverage known correlations or constraints (linearity, graph, etc.) LinTS, KernelUCB, Graph Bandits
Exploration Safety / Risk Aversion Avoid risky arms unless confidence improves CVaR bandits, Safe-UCB
Personalization / Adaptation Adapt to user/task/environment specifics Meta-bandits, Contextual TS

Algorithm BanditLearner:

1. Initialize:
    - Define the arm space A
    - Set priors OR confidence bounds for each arm a ∈ A
    - If structured: set up model assumptions (e.g., linear, Gaussian process, graphs)
    - If contextual: prepare feature extractor φ(x)

2. For t = 1 to T do:
    a. Observe context x_t (if contextual)
    
    b. Estimate:
        - Use data/history to update:
            • Posterior distribution (Bayesian)
            • Confidence intervals (Frequentist)
            • Preference/utility models (Ordinal)
        - Calculate scores:
            • UCB = estimate + bonus
            • TS: sample reward from posterior
            • EXP3: compute softmax-weighted probabilities
            
    c. Select arm a_t:
        - Use score-based selection:
            • Greedy / ε-greedy / Sampling
            • Top-k (combinatorial) / argmax

    d. Play arm a_t and receive feedback:
        - Full reward, partial feedback, ordinal, delayed, etc.

    e. Update:
        - Incorporate new feedback into:
            • Belief distributions
            • Empirical estimates
            • Model parameters
            
    f. [Optional] Constraints / Meta-Adjustments:
        - Budget checks
        - Privacy noise injection
        - Batched update logic
        - Risk thresholds

3. End

Let me know when you’d like to:

  • Add interactive sliders or plots to show objective evolution (e.g., effect of ε in ε-greedy).
  • Dive into specific algorithms’ math and simulations.
  • Illustrate how objectives differ for each bandit subclass interactively.

Would you like the next section to be: 🔍 A walk-through of classic bandit algorithms and their parameter intuitions? 🧪 A sandbox setup for visual experiments?

You can copy the markdown above directly into your blog or let me update it in the canvas document if you’d prefer!
entry.summary: How different objective functions reshape exploration, regret, risk, and decision-making goals in bandit problems.

entry.title: A Bird’s-Eye View of Multi-Armed Bandits

link.title: A Bird’s-Eye View of Multi-Armed Bandits

entry.content:
A unified top-down overview of the Multi-Armed Bandit landscape — from motivation and intuition to formal structure and experimental interactivity.

🧭 Introduction

Imagine you’re a doctor testing different treatments, a website testing ad designs, or an e-commerce platform choosing what product to feature. Every decision involves uncertainty and a goal: maximize reward while learning from experience. This is the essence of Multi-Armed Bandits (MABs).

This blog focuses not on deriving regret bounds or proving theorems, but rather:

  • Understanding the motivation and intuition behind MAB formulations
  • Exploring real-world scenarios that shaped these variants
  • Illustrating how algorithms behave — and sometimes fail
  • Offering interactive visualizations to deepen understanding

📜 A Historical and Cross-Disciplinary Perspective

The term “multi-armed bandit” comes from the analogy of a gambler facing several slot machines (each with unknown payout rates). It was first formalized by Thompson (1933), and later studied deeply by Robbins (1952) and others.

Over decades, MABs have become foundational across domains:

  • Statistics: experimental design, A/B testing
  • Economics: pricing and auctions
  • Reinforcement Learning: exploration-exploitation tradeoff
  • Causal Inference: identifying intervention effects
  • Cognitive Science: modeling human curiosity and exploration

🧱 Taxonomy of Bandit Problems

We categorize MAB extensions into six broad families:

Category Core Extension Examples
Feedback Variants How the learner receives feedback Partial, Delayed, Preference-based
Structural Variants Structure in actions or rewards Combinatorial, Linear, GP
Contextual Variants Extra information at decision time Contextual, Meta-contextual
Temporal Variants Environment dynamics over time Nonstationary, Restless, Rotting
Strategic / Adversarial Strategic agents or adversarial rewards Adversarial, Multi-agent
Constraint-driven Constraints on safety, fairness, budget Safe, Budgeted, Fair, Sleeping

🧠 Formalism, Intuition, and Examples

🎯 Standard MAB Setup

The heart and soul of the MAB problem is to intelligently balance exploration and exploitation to maximize payoff over time. Exploration is reducing uncertainty over the payoffs for the arms, however not reducing uncertainty globally but rather reducing uncertainty for arms which have potentially high payoffs. Intuition for this could be in spotify music for example, if we know that a user does not like a particular song with some certainty there is no utility in investigating how much the user dislikes that song. From an information theoretic sense, the bandit problem is constructing experiments which minimise the entropy over the optimal arm, that is conduct informative experiments \(I(\Pr(a^*);\mathcal{E})\) where \(\Pr(a^*)\) is the probability of optimal arms

The standard bandit framework considers, numerical scalar feedback. But feedback could be a vector when the user cares about multiple objectives, the bandit could recieve comparative / ordinal feedback or preferential feedback. The feedback might be corrupted where there is missingness in data (missingness at random or biased missingness) or delayed.

The optimality of an arm is subjective, arms could have associated costs and bandits might be interested in finding cost effective but satisficing arms, the bandits goal might put safety first or might try to find the best arm in the least amount of time.

Depending on the objectives of the bandit the strategy for playing arms will evolve. The general principle or goal for bandits remain the same, based on underlying structure of the problem, the

While the standard setting considers,

At each round ( t = 1, 2, \dots, T ):

  • The agent selects an arm ( a_t \in \mathcal{A} )
  • Receives reward ( r_t \sim \mathcal{D}_{a_t} )
  • Goal: maximize expected cumulative reward or minimize regret

Objectives:

  • Cumulative Regret:
    ( R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T (r^* - r_t) )
  • Simple Regret:
    ( R_T^{\text{simple}} = r^* - \mathbb{E}[r_{\hat{a}}] )

🧩 Feedback Variants

Dueling Bandits

  • Feedback: preference between two arms
  • Objective: find best arm using pairwise comparisons
  • Use case: UI design, recommendation systems
  • Generalization: Ordinal Bandits (rankings, top-k feedback)

Partial Monitoring

  • Feedback signal is indirect or noisy
  • Often modeled via feedback graphs
  • Generalization: Graph Feedback Bandits

🧩 Structural Variants

Combinatorial Bandits

  • Action = subset of base arms
  • Reward = sum or function over subset
  • Use case: ad selection, sensor networks
  • Generalization: Submodular Bandits

Linear Bandits

  • Each arm has a feature vector ( x ), reward ( r = x^\top \theta + \eta )
  • Use case: portfolio selection
  • Generalization: Generalized Linear Bandits, Non-linear Bandits (e.g., neural)

🧩 Contextual Variants

Contextual Bandits

  • Observe context ( x_t ), choose action ( a_t ), get reward ( r_t )
  • Use case: personalized content, dynamic pricing
  • Generalization: Meta-Contextual Bandits (transfer across tasks)

🧩 Temporal Variants

Nonstationary Bandits

  • Reward distributions change over time
  • Use case: recommendation systems, financial markets
  • Generalization: Change-point models

Restless Bandits

  • Arms evolve even when not played
  • Use case: network routing, resource allocation
  • Generalization: POMDP Bandits

🧩 Strategic / Adversarial Variants

Adversarial Bandits

  • No assumptions on reward generation
  • Worst-case regret bounds
  • Generalization: Bandits with Switching Costs

Multi-agent Bandits

  • Multiple learners interacting
  • Use case: federated learning
  • Generalization: Bandit Games (game-theoretic dynamics)

🧩 Constraint-driven Bandits

Safe Bandits

  • Ensure low probability of catastrophic outcomes
  • Use case: clinical trials
  • Generalization: Constrained MDP Bandits

Budgeted Bandits

  • Limited total cost or rounds
  • Use case: online ad allocation

Fair Bandits

  • Enforce fairness across groups or actions
  • Use case: hiring platforms
  • Generalization: Group-aware Fairness, Long-term Fairness

Sleeping Bandits

  • Availability of arms varies per round
  • Use case: edge computing, ride-sharing
  • Generalization: Opportunistic Bandits

🌱 Generalizations and Beyond

Each variant can be extended further by combining aspects:

  • Contextual + Structural → Contextual Combinatorial Bandits
  • Safe + Nonstationary → Constrained Adaptive Bandits
  • Ordinal + Budgeted → Budget-aware Preference Learning

Many problems lie on this continuum. Understanding these intersections helps in modeling realistic, rich environments.

🚨 When Algorithms Fail

Even the best-known algorithms can fail under misspecification:

  • Thompson Sampling with poor priors
  • UCB in nonstationary environments
  • Epsilon-Greedy under sparse rewards

We’ll illustrate these behaviors via interactive simulations.

🧪 Interactive Simulations

Coming soon: tweak ε in ε-Greedy, visualize Thompson Sampling posteriors, simulate contextual scenarios.

📚 References

```bibtex @article{thompson1933likelihood, title={On the likelihood that one unknown probability exceeds another in view of the evidence of two samples}, author={Thompson, William R.}, journal={Biometrika}, volume={25}, number={3/4}, pages={285–294}, year={1933}, publisher={Oxford University Press} } …
entry.summary: A structured exploration of bandit problem types, their assumptions, modeling frameworks, and real-world applicability.

entry.title: A Bird’s-Eye View of Causal Inference Frameworks

link.title: A Bird’s-Eye View of Causal Inference Frameworks

entry.content:
A unified map of the leading frameworks in causal inference: their philosophical roots, assumptions, tools, and the practical problems they best address.

🧭 Overview

Causal inference is about answering the question: “What will happen if we do X?”—not just “What is correlated with X?” This post presents a high-level view of the major causal inference frameworks, highlighting their assumptions, modeling techniques, and when to use them.

🧱 Major Frameworks

Framework Key Thinker Tools Assumptions Modeling Use Case
SCM (Do-Calculus) Pearl DAGs, Do-calculus No unmeasured confounding (or model it) Graphs, functional relationships When causal structure is known or learnable
Potential Outcomes (PO) Rubin ATE, ATT, RCTs Ignorability, SUTVA Matching, weighting, randomization Experimental or quasi-experimental settings
Instrumental Variables (IV) Angrist, Imbens 2SLS, LATE Exclusion, monotonicity Econometric 2-stage regressions Natural experiments
RDD Campbell Local regression Sharp/fuzzy assignment threshold Discontinuity-based estimation Policies or cutoffs
DiD Econometricians Panel regressions Parallel trends Time-series + control groups Policy evaluation
Bayesian Causal Nets Spirtes, Glymour Probabilistic DAGs Markov, faithfulness Learning from conditional independence Causal discovery
Counterfactual SEMs Pearl Functional equations + counterfactuals Model-based counterfactuals Simulation + SCM Transportability, policy modeling

🧠 Key Differences

🔍 Assumptions

  • SCM: Assumes accurate knowledge of the causal DAG.
  • PO: Relies on ignorability and SUTVA.
  • IV: Requires a valid instrument that affects outcome only via treatment.
  • RDD: Requires treatment to hinge on a known threshold.
  • DiD: Assumes treated and untreated would follow same trend absent treatment.

🧪 Interventions & Counterfactuals

  • SCM: Explicit do(X) notation for interventions.
  • PO: Defines counterfactuals ( Y(1), Y(0) ), even if not observable.
  • IV/RDD/DiD: Implicitly model the counterfactual world via design or structure.

⚙️ Modeling Techniques

  • SCM: DAGs, do-calculus, backdoor/frontdoor rules.
  • PO: Propensity score matching, inverse probability weighting, stratification.
  • IV: Two-stage least squares (2SLS), Wald estimators.
  • RDD: Local polynomial regression near threshold.
  • DiD: Fixed effects panel regression on treatment timing.

🔧 Use Case Guide

Scenario Recommended Framework
Randomized control trials (RCTs) Potential Outcomes
Observational study, rich covariates SCM or PO
Valid instrument available IV
Treatment by score threshold RDD
Policy change across groups and time DiD
Need to simulate interventions/counterfactuals SCM or SEM

🌱 Modern & Hybrid Approaches

🌳 ML + Causality

  • Causal Forests: Estimate individual treatment effects.
  • Double Machine Learning (DML): Correct bias using ML for nuisance terms.
  • Neural Causal Models: Learn DAGs or simulate counterfactuals.
  • Automated Discovery: NOTEARS, GES, FCI algorithms for causal structure.

⚠️ Limitations

  • SCM: Mis-specified DAG leads to incorrect conclusions.
  • PO: Counterfactuals are untestable without strong assumptions.
  • IV: Instruments often violate exclusion or monotonicity.
  • RDD/DiD: Assumptions like parallel trends are often untestable.

Causal inference is powerful, but assumptions must be contextually justified and ideally sensitivity-tested.

📚 References

::: bib

  • Pearl, Judea. Causality: Models, Reasoning and Inference. Cambridge University Press, 2009.
  • Imbens, Guido W., and Donald B. Rubin. Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences. Cambridge, 2015.
  • Hernán, Miguel A., and James M. Robins. Causal Inference: What If. CRC Press, 2020.
  • Peters, Jonas, et al. Elements of Causal Inference. MIT Press, 2017. :::

Thanks for reading. You can reach out here or explore more posts on causal inference.
entry.summary: A structured overview of major causal inference paradigms, their assumptions, techniques, and practical applicability.

entry.title: Smarter Decisions with Multi-Armed Bandits
entry.content:
🎰 Learning by Trying: Why Bandits Matter

Picture this.

You’re running a clinical trial for a new drug. Patients are coming in daily. You have multiple treatments to choose from, but no clear winner yet. Every choice you make affects a real person — and every wrong choice is a missed opportunity to help. You don’t want to guess. You want to learn as you go, adapt based on feedback, and improve decisions over time.

This isn’t just medicine. The same challenge shows up in personalized ads, recommendation systems, autonomous agents, A/B testing, and even education platforms. In all these domains, we face a common dilemma:

How do we make the best possible decisions while still learning what the best is?

This is the heart of the Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) problem — a deceptively simple framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty.

🎯 What This Blog Is — And Isn’t

There are plenty of excellent resources out there that rigorously prove regret bounds and derive tight concentration inequalities for bandit algorithms. If you want the math-heavy deep dive, I’ll link to them below.

But that’s not what this post is about.

This blog is for those who want to understand the motivations and intuitions behind the objectives that drive bandit learning. It’s for those curious about:

  • Why do we talk about “regret”?
  • What does “exploration vs. exploitation” really mean in practice?
  • What are the different kinds of feedback we might get?
  • When do popular algorithms like UCB or Thompson Sampling shine — and when do they break?

We’ll cover the design principles, edge cases, and intuitions that shaped this field, and walk through how different objectives (like cumulative vs. simple regret) arise from real-world needs.

🧭 Where We’re Headed

Here’s what we’ll explore:

  • Motivations behind MABs: sequential learning, decision pressure, feedback sparsity.
  • Objectives like cumulative regret, simple regret, PAC guarantees — and when each matters.
  • Different types of feedback: stochastic, adversarial, contextual, ordinal.
  • Algorithmic insights: how UCB, Thompson Sampling, and others work (and why).
  • Where they break: edge cases and mismatched assumptions.
  • The bigger picture: how MABs relate to RL, optimization, and learning theory.

My hope is that by the end of this post, you’ll not only know what a bandit algorithm does, but also why it does it, and when you should care.

Let’s start at the beginning — with a gambler, a handful of slot machines, and a question that’s deceptively simple:
Which one should I pull next?

Multi-Armed Bandits (MABs) offer a powerful way to make sequential decisions under uncertainty, where each action provides feedback that can improve future choices. From online advertising to clinical trials, this framework appears in some of the most high-impact applications of AI.

🎰 Why Are They Called “Bandits”?

The term “multi-armed bandit” comes from the classic slot machine analogy — also known as a one-armed bandit. Each machine (or arm) offers an unknown probability of payout. You can pull one arm at a time and want to win as much as possible over time.

This metaphor was formalized in the 1950s by statistician Herbert Robbins, whose foundational work focused on adaptive allocation and regret minimization. The core challenge? Balancing exploration (trying new arms) with exploitation (using the best-known option so far).

🔁 Sequential Decision-Making: The Core Setting

MABs represent a minimal model of online learning where:

  • You must repeatedly choose between multiple actions.
  • Each action gives limited feedback.
  • There’s no full supervision, only rewards from chosen actions.

This structure is ideal when data is not fully labeled or feedback is expensive.

🎯 What Are We Optimizing For?

1. Cumulative Regret

The most common objective: maximize total reward over time, or equivalently, minimize cumulative regret.

\[ R_T = T \cdot \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^T \mu_{a_t} \]

Where \( \mu^* \) is the true mean reward of the best arm, and \( a_t \) is the arm pulled at time \( t \).

Used in:

  • Online advertising
  • A/B testing
  • Recommendation systems

2. Simple Regret

The goal here is to identify the best arm, not necessarily to accumulate reward during learning.

This is key when:

  • Only the final decision matters (e.g., best drug).
  • The cost of exploration is acceptable, but accuracy is critical.

3. PAC (Probably Approximately Correct)

We want to identify an arm that’s within ( \epsilon ) of optimal with high probability ( 1 - \delta ). This blends sample efficiency with statistical guarantees.

4. Risk-Sensitive Objectives

In high-stakes environments, it’s not enough to optimize the mean. Instead, we focus on:

  • Variance-aware strategies
  • Minimax regret
  • CVaR (Conditional Value at Risk)

Applications: finance, autonomous systems, healthcare.

📊 What Kind of Feedback Can We Get?

The classic setting assumes stochastic numeric rewards, but real-world feedback is richer and messier.

✅ Stochastic Numerical

  • Each arm gives a random reward from a fixed distribution.
  • Algorithms: UCB, Thompson Sampling

✅ Binary Feedback

  • Especially used in click-through data, where reward ∈ {0, 1}.
  • Simple but powerful in large-scale recommender systems.

✅ Ordinal / Preference-Based

  • Feedback is comparative: “Arm A > Arm B”.
  • Useful in:
    • Human preferences
    • Ranking problems
    • Crowdsourcing

A popular framework: Dueling Bandits

✅ Adversarial Feedback

  • No assumptions about reward generation.
  • The worst-case scenario is considered.
  • Algorithms: EXP3, Hedge

Used in:

  • Network routing
  • Cybersecurity
  • Competitive markets

✅ Contextual Feedback

  • Each decision comes with a context vector (e.g., user profile).
  • The best arm depends on context.

Applications:

  • Personalized recommendations
  • Adaptive user interfaces

📈 Bandit Algorithms at a Glance

```plotly { “data”: [ { “x”: [“UCB”, “Thompson Sampling”, “EXP3”, “LinUCB”, “Dueling Bandits”], “y”: [0.1, 0.05, 0.3, 0.07, 0.15], “type”: “bar” } ], “layout”: { “title”: { “text”: “Hypothetical Regret Comparison of Bandit Algorithms” }, “yaxis”: { “title”: “Average Regret” } } }
entry.summary: Explore the theory, objectives, feedback types, and real-world use cases of Multi-Armed Bandits in sequential decision-making.
